main_damage_causes: Compression of Colin's introduction removes his sustained 'talkative shadow' characterization and his one moment of courage (standing up to Malfoy), reducing him from a specific comic type to a generic camera-gag character

Absence of Colin's death in the Deathly Hallows films eliminates the only meaningful arc payoff for the character - the Muggle-born fan who was attacked for existing dying as a fighter - leaving his story without resolution or thematic weight
Loss of Dumbledore's 'the question is not who but how' line during the Petrification scene removes a subtle but important piece of mystery foreshadowing tied directly to Colin's role as a victim

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 1 (Great Hall introduction) - Colin's introduction is compressed and reordered
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Colin first approaches Harry in the courtyard with a camera, goes bright red, asks tentatively for a photo, explains his Muggle background and excitement about magic, asks Harry to sign it - a full scene establishing his earnest fan worship, social awkwardness, and Muggle-born wonder. Malfoy then mocks Harry for 'giving out signed photos', Colin pipes up to defend Harry ('You're just jealous'), and Lockhart swoops in.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Colin appears at the Great Hall lunch table, flashes his camera at Harry, delivers a compressed version of his Muggle-background speech, and asks for a photo. The Malfoy courtyard confrontation, Colin's brave 'You're just jealous' line, and the Lockhart signed-photo humiliation scene are all absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's courtyard scene does triple duty: it establishes Colin's personality (eager, brave enough to pipe up at Malfoy), Harry's embarrassment dynamic with him, and the Lockhart humiliation. The film collapses this to a brief comic flash. Colin's one moment of genuine courage - standing up to Malfoy - is entirely lost, flattening him to pure comic relief.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 1 (Great Hall) - Colin's 'talkative shadow' characterization absent
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The book devotes significant space to Colin memorizing Harry's schedule, greeting him six or seven times a day, following him uninvited to Quidditch practice, asking relentless questions about Quidditch rules all the way to the pitch - establishing him as an obsessive, unstoppable presence Harry cannot shake ('like having an extremely talkative shadow').
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: No equivalent scene exists. Colin appears briefly at the Great Hall and then at the Quidditch match/hospital wing. The sustained stalker-fan dynamic across multiple days is entirely absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The 'talkative shadow' material is the primary vehicle for Colin's characterization as a specific type of overwhelming, well-meaning fan. Without it, he is a one-note camera gag rather than a fully realized comic presence with a consistent behavioral pattern.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 (Quidditch match/slug scene) - Colin's camera intrusion on Ron's slug-vomiting
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the book, Colin runs down from the stands after Ron starts vomiting slugs and tries to photograph it ('Can you hold him still, Harry?'), showing his oblivious enthusiasm overriding basic social decency - Harry angrily tells him to get out of the way.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This beat is retained in the film ('Wow! Can you hold him still, Harry?!') and Harry's 'Get out of the way, Colin!' response is present. This is one of the more faithful moments.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This beat is largely faithful. Minor compression only - the book has Colin 'dancing alongside them' and being more persistent. Minimal damage.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 2 (Hospital wing after Quidditch) - Colin's camera during Lockhart's botched bone removal
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In the book, Colin is present in the crowd when Lockhart removes Harry's bones and begins 'clicking away madly' as Harry's arm deflates - a grotesque, darkly comic moment showing Colin's total inability to read the room, photographing Harry's horror and humiliation.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Colin appears in the hospital wing after Harry has already been brought there, says 'That was brilliant today, Harry! Brilliant!' and is shooed out by Madam Pomfrey. The camera-clicking during the bone-removal horror is absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book's version is a sharper, darker comic beat - Colin photographing Harry's physical horror is more disturbing and funnier than a generic 'brilliant!' line. The film softens him into a straightforward admirer rather than someone whose enthusiasm is genuinely inappropriate.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 1 (Howler scene) - Colin snapping photos of Ron's Howler humiliation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: The book does not have Colin present at the Howler scene in the Great Hall in this way - this is a film-only addition.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Colin Creevey snaps photos of Ron's Howler humiliation. This is a film invention.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is actually a faithful extension of Colin's character - using his camera indiscriminately. Minimal damage, if any. Not an unfaithful beat.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film Scene 3 (End celebration) - Colin snapping photos at the feast
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Colin is Petrified for most of the second half of the book and is revived by Mandrake Draught. His return is noted as part of the general revival. He does not have a specific scene at the end feast.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Colin is shown snapping photos at the end-of-year feast celebration, a brief background appearance.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Faithful in spirit - using Colin's camera as a visual shorthand for his character. No meaningful damage.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film - Colin's Petrification scene (Hospital wing, night)
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Harry witnesses Colin's Petrification from behind his hospital curtain - Colin is brought in as a statue, camera still clutched to his eye. Dumbledore opens the camera and steam hisses out. McGonagall wonders if he photographed his attacker. Dumbledore's response ('The question is not who... the question is how') is a key mystery beat. Harry is a hidden witness to this entire exchange.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The scene is largely retained: Colin is brought in Petrified with camera, McGonagall asks about a photo of the attacker, Dumbledore opens the camera (jet of steam), and delivers a modified line: 'He would surely be dead' without the camera. The mystery framing is shifted - Dumbledore's 'the question is not who but how' is replaced with a more direct statement about danger.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Dumbledore's 'the question is not who, the question is how' is a significant piece of foreshadowing and characterization - it shows Dumbledore already thinking about the mechanism (the basilisk's gaze) rather than the perpetrator. The film replaces this with a more generic danger statement, losing a subtle but important beat. The scene otherwise functions adequately.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film - Absence of Colin's role in GoF (Triwizard/champion escort scene)
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: In GoF, Colin is sent to fetch Harry from Potions for the champions' photo session - he nervously faces down Snape, goes pink, squeaks at Snape about Mr. Bagman's instructions, and walks Harry to the room chatting excitedly. This shows Colin in a new context: still enthusiastic but now a third-year with a small errand role, still in awe of Harry but also functioning independently.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The GoF film does not include Colin in this role. The escort to the photo session happens differently or is omitted.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is a minor but charming scene showing Colin's development across books. Its absence is a small loss but not structurally damaging given Colin's secondary role.

damaging_scenes.scene: Film - Absence of Colin's death in Deathly Hallows
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Colin dies in the Battle of Hogwarts despite being underage - he sneaked back in. Harry sees his body being carried by Neville and Oliver Wood. He is described as 'tiny in death.' He is listed among the fallen alongside Fred, Tonks, Lupin. This is the culmination of his arc: the enthusiastic Muggle-born first-year who idolized Harry dies fighting for the same cause.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The DH films do not include Colin's death or body. He is absent from the Battle of Hogwarts entirely in the films.
damaging_scenes.explanation: Colin's death is the payoff of his entire characterization arc. The eager, camera-wielding Muggle-born fan who was Petrified in CoS for being who he was dies years later fighting back. 'He was tiny in death' is one of the most affecting lines in DH. His absence from the films means this arc has no conclusion, and the thematic weight of a Muggle-born dying for the cause he believed in is lost. This is the most structurally significant damage to the character across the saga.
